raw:
How I Designed the /writing-tuner Claude Code Skill

Building tools that shape how AI writes is a strange recursive exercise. You're using language to teach a machine about language preferences, then asking it to apply those preferences back to you. That's the core tension behind /writing-tuner — a Claude Code skill I built to iteratively refine AI writing style through direct, word-level feedback.

The Problem

Most AI writing sounds the same. You can tell it to "write in my voice" and it'll nod along, then produce the same bland, agreeable prose it always does. Prompt engineering helps, but it's fragile. You end up with a wall of instructions that drift every time you start a new conversation.

I wanted something more durable. A system where feedback compounds — where telling the AI "don't use that word" once means it actually stops using that word, not just in this conversation, but in every future one.

The Architecture

The skill works as an orchestration layer. Claude Code acts as the session manager, coordinating between a Node.js CLI, a browser-based annotation interface, and a markdown style guide that grows over time.

The loop is simple: generate a draft, annotate it in the browser, extract preferences, update the guide, then proof the result. Each cycle tightens the guide. The guide itself is a versioned markdown file — human-readable, portable, and easy to paste into any system prompt.

I chose browser-based annotation over inline CLI feedback because clicking on specific words is faster and more precise than describing what you don't like. The annotation server renders your text and lets you tag individual words or phrases as good, bad, or "replace with this." That granularity matters.

Key Design Decisions

The confidence system was important. Each preference starts at "low" confidence and gets promoted as you reinforce it across rounds. This prevents a single offhand annotation from permanently warping the guide.

Versioning was non-negotiable. Every save creates a numbered snapshot. You can always roll back. The guide-latest.md symlink means you can resume from wherever you left off without remembering version numbers.

I also built in anti-voice tracking — not just what you want, but what you explicitly don't want. Most style guides only capture the positive space. But knowing that someone never uses "leverage" as a verb or hates semicolons in blog posts is just as valuable as knowing they prefer short sentences.

What I Learned

The hardest part wasn't the technical architecture. It was designing the feedback loop to feel natural. Early versions asked too many questions. Later versions didn't ask enough. The current design leans on the browser annotation as the primary input channel, with Claude Code handling the bookkeeping.

The other surprise: the guide format matters more than I expected. A style guide that's easy for humans to read and edit is also easier for the AI to follow. Markdown with clear section headers and concrete examples beats a JSON blob every time.

If you want to try it, the skill is open and the guides are portable. Start a session, annotate a few rounds, and you'll have a style guide that actually reflects how you write — not how the AI thinks everyone writes.
